summary: No backward scrolling in a Torah scroll. HP furled scroll and declared 'more is written here' to protect the scroll's honor. Eight blessings and beautiful Torah scrolls displayed. Dispute: Rabbi Eliezer vs Rabbi Akiva on mussaf offering timing.

display.whats: The HP may not roll a Torah scroll backward (from the end toward the beginning) when moving between passages - an established principle in Torah reading.
display.hint: What is the practical reason behind the prohibition of rolling the scroll backward?
display.title: No Rolling Backward: The Torah Scroll's One-Way Journey

learning.ahaMoment: The backward-rolling prohibition protects the congregation's dignity - making them wait while the scroll is laboriously re-wound is disrespectful to the public assembly.
learning.memoryAnchor: No rolling backward - the scroll only moves forward.
learning.learnerQuestion: Is it permitted to roll a Torah scroll backward during a public reading to reach an earlier passage?
learning.coreTension: Sometimes reaching a specific passage requires rolling backward through the scroll. Why should direction matter for what seems like a practical logistics question?
learning.coreMove: The Gemara establishes that rolling a Torah scroll backward is prohibited during a reading. Rolling backward makes the congregation wait unnecessarily. The scroll must always move forward - the HP's declaration after reading Lev. 23 by heart resolves this practical constraint for the Yom Kippur ceremony.

takeaway.text: One may not roll a Torah scroll backward during a reading - the scroll must always advance forward. This formal prohibition protects the congregation from unnecessary waiting.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may think backward scrolling is merely inconvenient, not prohibited. The ruling establishes it as a formal prohibition derived from respect for the congregation.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: No rolling a Torah scroll backward
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: One may not roll a Torah scroll backward - from the end toward the beginning. This is relevant to the HP's Yom Kippur reading.

halachic: prohibition of backward scrolling in Torah reading
theological: honor of congregation in public Torah reading

misconceptions.misconception: A Torah scroll can be rolled in either direction as needed
misconceptions.correction: One may not roll a Torah scroll backward during a reading - the scroll must move forward. This protects the congregation from unnecessary waiting.

quizSeeds.question: What is the rule about rolling a Torah scroll backward during a reading?
quizSeeds.answer: It is prohibited - one may not roll the scroll from the end toward the beginning. The scroll can only advance forward during a public reading.

sugyot.finalRuling: Rolling a Torah scroll backward is prohibited.

display.whats: After the Torah reading the HP rolled the scroll, pressed it to his chest, and declared: 'More than what I have read is written here' - to prevent the congregation from thinking the third passage (Lev. 23) was missing from the scroll.
display.hint: Why would the congregation think the Lev. 23 passage was missing from the scroll?
display.title: The HP's Declaration: Protecting the Scroll's Honor

learning.ahaMoment: The scroll was pressed to the HP's chest in a physical gesture of intimacy with the Torah - a moment of public honor for both the HP and the scroll before the assembled nation.
learning.memoryAnchor: HP presses rolled scroll to chest and declares: 'More is written here than I read.'
learning.learnerQuestion: After reading Leviticus 23 by heart, what did the HP do to protect the Torah scroll's honor, and why was this necessary?
learning.coreTension: The HP read Lev. 23 by heart, not from the scroll. The congregation might conclude that portion was missing from the scroll - a serious implied denigration. How was this misimpression prevented?
learning.coreMove: The HP pressed the rolled scroll to his chest and declared 'more is written here than what I read.' This declaration was legally necessary - without it, the congregation might conclude the Torah scroll was defective, constituting a public denigration of the Torah.

takeaway.text: When Torah is read by heart, a declaration is legally required that the scroll contains more than what was recited - protecting the Torah's honor and preventing the impression of a defective text.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may treat the declaration as ceremonial. The Gemara establishes it is legally necessary: without it, the silence would imply the scroll is missing a passage, which constitutes a public degradation of the Torah.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: HP furled scroll and pressed it to his chest
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: After the readings, the HP rolled up the scroll, pressed it to his chest, and declared: 'More than I have read before you is written here.'

halachic: declaration after by-heart reading to protect scroll honor
theological: physical intimacy with Torah as public gesture
narrative: HP holding the Torah to his chest before the nation

misconceptions.misconception: The HP's declaration was merely a formality
misconceptions.correction: The declaration was legally necessary - without it, the congregation might conclude that Lev. 23 was missing from the scroll, constituting a public denigration of the Torah.

visualizableElements.description: HP in white vestments pressing rolled Torah scroll to his chest before the assembled congregation

quizSeeds.question: Why did the HP press the Torah scroll to his chest after the reading and make a declaration?
quizSeeds.answer: To protect the scroll's honor - since he read Lev. 23 by heart, the congregation might think that portion was missing from the scroll. He declared 'more is written here' to prevent that impression.

sugyot.finalRuling: After the Torah reading, the HP rolled the scroll and pressed it to his chest, declaring 'more than what I read is written here' to protect the scroll's honor.

display.whats: Eight blessings are required for the by-heart reading (not just seven for the standard reading). The Gemara explains the reason - and discusses displaying Torah scrolls to honor the congregation and the commandment of beautifying mitzvot.
display.hint: Why does reading from memory require an additional blessing compared to reading from the scroll?
display.title: Eight Blessings and the Beauty of Torah Scrolls

learning.ahaMoment: The practice of displaying the Torah scrolls to the congregation isn't just ceremonial - it connects to the principle of beautifying commandments (hidur mitzvah). The physical beauty of Torah is itself a religious statement.
learning.memoryAnchor: Eight blessings + beautiful Torah scrolls displayed = honoring God through beauty of the mitzvah.
learning.learnerQuestion: What are the HP's eight blessings after the Torah reading, and what principle does the practice of displaying Torah scrolls to the congregation reflect?
learning.coreTension: The eight blessings conclude the Torah reading ceremony. Torah scrolls were also displayed to the congregation. Is this purely ceremonial, or does it reflect a deeper principle about how to serve God?
learning.coreMove: The eight blessings are enumerated and Torah scrolls were displayed to the congregation as hiddur mitzvah - beautifying the commandment. The source is Exodus 15:2: 've'anveihu' means to glorify God by performing commandments with beautiful objects - beautiful Torah scroll, beautiful lulav, beautiful parchment and ink.

takeaway.text: Beautifying sacred objects - beautiful Torah scrolls, beautiful lulav - is itself a Torah value derived from 've'anveihu.' The HP's ceremony makes this principle public and visible.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may see displaying Torah scrolls as merely ceremonial. The Gemara grounds it in hiddur mitzvah - beautification is not optional but an expression of how one honors God in performing commandments.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Eight blessings required for by-heart reading
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The by-heart reading requires eight blessings (not seven for the standard scroll reading) because an additional blessing is needed when reading without a scroll.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Beautiful Torah scrolls displayed to congregation
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Torah scrolls were displayed to the assembled congregation before the reading - to show the honor of the scroll and the beauty of its owner who beautified himself in the commandment.

argumentFlow.label: Reason: Beautifying oneself in commandments - hidur mitzvah
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Based on Exodus 15:2 'This is my God and I will glorify Him' - beautify yourself before God with beautiful commandments: a beautiful lulav, a beautiful Torah scroll, beautiful parchment, beautiful ink, a skilled scribe.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Scrolls were brought from the day before Yom Kippur
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Torah scrolls were brought to the Temple from the day before Yom Kippur to be ready for display and reading.

theological: 'This is my God and I will glorify Him'
narrative: beautiful Torah scrolls brought to Temple for Yom Kippur

misconceptions.misconception: Displaying Torah scrolls is a modern practice
misconceptions.correction: The practice of displaying Torah scrolls to the congregation was established in Temple times as part of the Yom Kippur ceremony, connected to the principle of beautifying commandments.

visualizableElements.description: Beautiful Torah scrolls displayed to congregation, then HP reads from one while declaring the others contain more

quizSeeds.question: What is the biblical source for beautifying Torah scrolls and commandments?
quizSeeds.answer: Exodus 15:2 - 'This is my God and I will glorify Him' (ve'anveihu) - interpreted as beautifying yourself before God through beautiful commandments: beautiful lulav, beautiful Torah scroll, beautiful parchment and ink.

sugyot.finalRuling: Eight blessings are recited. Torah scrolls were displayed to the congregation as hidur mitzvah - beautifying the commandment.

display.whats: The HP prostrated himself eight times - once for each of the eight blessings. The exact count and sequence is derived from the Mishnah's list.
display.hint: What is the significance of prostrating once for each blessing rather than once at the end?
display.title: Eight Prostrations: Embodied Submission at Each Blessing

learning.ahaMoment: Eight prostrations in public view was itself a powerful religious statement - the most elevated person in Israel repeatedly flattening himself before God, demonstrating that true greatness is measured by submission.
learning.memoryAnchor: Eight blessings = eight prostrations. Greatest man in Israel, eight times on the floor.
learning.learnerQuestion: How many times did the HP prostrate himself during the Yom Kippur Torah reading ceremony, and what was the theological message of repeated prostration?
learning.coreTension: The HP recited eight blessings. Was his prostration one act at the end, or did each blessing conclude with its own physical act of submission?
learning.coreMove: The HP prostrated eight times - once after each blessing. The repeated prostrations in public view, while wearing the magnificent vestments of the HP, created a powerful paradox: the greatest man in Israel repeatedly and publicly humbling himself before God.

takeaway.text: Eight blessings, eight prostrations - each individual act of honor concludes with embodied submission. The HP's repeated public humility was itself the theological message of Yom Kippur.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume one final prostration at the close of all blessings. The pattern is one prostration per blessing - each individual blessing is concluded with its own physical act of submission.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: HP prostrated eight times
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: The HP prostrated himself eight times - once for each of the eight blessings he recited after the Torah reading.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Commandment not to pass over active obligations
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The principle 'do not pass over commandments' - one may not ignore an active obligation in front of you to pursue another. However seeing Torah scrolls on display is itself a commandment.

theological: prostration as expression of total submission
the HP as model of humility
narrative: HP prostrating eight times before assembled nation

misconceptions.misconception: The HP prostrated only once at the end of all eight blessings
misconceptions.correction: He prostrated eight times - once after each blessing. Each blessing concluded with a prostration.

visualizableElements.description: HP reciting eight blessings in sequence, prostrating fully after each one before the assembled congregation

quizSeeds.question: How many times did the HP prostrate himself during the Torah reading ceremony on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: Eight times - once after each of the eight blessings he recited.

sugyot.finalRuling: The HP prostrated eight times - once for each of the eight blessings after the Torah reading.

display.whats: Witnessing the HP's reading and prostration was a unique spiritual experience - those who saw it described it as incomparable. The service had both halachic precision and spiritual power.
display.hint: What made witnessing the HP's Yom Kippur ceremony uniquely powerful for the observers?
display.title: Nothing Compared to It: The Witness Reports on the HP's Prostration

learning.ahaMoment: Witnessing the HP prostrate while wearing the vestments of atonement, before the assembled nation on the holiest day of the year - this was the visible apex of Israel's relationship with God.
learning.memoryAnchor: Those who saw it said: nothing compared to it.
learning.learnerQuestion: What did those who witnessed the HP prostrating in his vestments on Yom Kippur report about the experience?
learning.coreTension: The Talmud preserves eyewitness reports that the experience was incomparable. What made the sight so overwhelming, and why is the experiential record worth preserving alongside legal data?
learning.coreMove: Witnesses reported that seeing the HP prostrate while wearing the magnificent vestments of the High Priesthood was unlike anything else - supreme human honor combined with utter submission to God. The mussaf offering sequence is also clarified: one lamb with the morning tamid, the remaining mussaf with the afternoon tamid.

takeaway.text: The combination of supreme dignity and complete submission made the HP's prostration uniquely powerful. Those who witnessed it preserved the memory as incomparable - the experiential record is itself part of what Torah transmits.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may focus on the halachic detail (mussaf timing). The Gemara also preserves the experiential witness as valuable in itself - the emotional and spiritual impact of the Temple service is part of the corpus, not just a sentimental footnote.

argumentFlow.label: Narrative: Witnessing the HP's reading was incomparable
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Those who witnessed the HP reading Torah and prostrating on Yom Kippur described it as an experience unlike any other.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Mishnah Ch. 8 begins - five afflictions of Yom Kippur
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: Mishnah Chapter 8: seven lambs of the mussaf - one is performed with the morning tamid (first vestment change), and others at the fifth immersion with the afternoon tamid.

halachic: service sequence for Yom Kippur mussaf
theological: spiritual power of witnessing the Temple service
narrative: eyewitness description of HP's reading ceremony

misconceptions.misconception: The Yom Kippur mussaf offerings were all performed at once
misconceptions.correction: The seven mussaf lambs were spread across the day: one with the morning tamid (first vestment change), and the rest with the afternoon tamid (fifth immersion).

visualizableElements.description: HP prostrating in white vestments before the assembled nation - eyewitnesses describing it as incomparable

quizSeeds.question: What is the correct timing for the Yom Kippur mussaf offerings?
quizSeeds.answer: One mussaf lamb is performed with the morning tamid (first vestment change); the remaining mussaf offerings are performed with the afternoon tamid (fifth immersion).

sugyot.finalRuling: Witnessing the HP's Torah reading ceremony was described as incomparable. The mussaf offerings are split across the day's immersion schedule.

display.whats: Mishnah Ch. 8 introduces the five Yom Kippur afflictions. The Gemara discusses the correct service sequence: dispute between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Akiva about when to perform the mussaf - before or after the day's service. Each position has scriptural support and practical concerns about the HP's strength.
display.hint: Why would performing all the mussaf offerings before the day's service be a problem?
display.title: R. Akiva's Service Order: Protecting the HP for the Essential Atonement

learning.ahaMoment: Rabbi Akiva's position shows sophisticated awareness of human limitations: if the HP is exhausted by the mussaf before the day's service, the essential atonement work might be compromised. Pragmatic concern for the HP's strength is itself a halachic consideration.
learning.memoryAnchor: R. Akiva: one mussaf lamb first, then day's service, then the rest. Don't tire the HP before the essential work.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why does Rabbi Akiva insist that only one mussaf lamb be performed before the day's essential Yom Kippur service, rearranging the apparent scriptural sequence?
learning.coreTension: Scripture's order seems to place the mussaf offerings before the essential atonement service. R. Akiva disrupts this. On what grounds can the sequence be rearranged, and why does he do so?
learning.coreMove: R. Akiva is concerned that performing all the mussaf offerings first would tire the HP before the essential atonement service - the day's primary purpose. He places one mussaf lamb first, then the essential service, then the remaining mussaf at the third immersion, and the afternoon tamid at the fifth.

takeaway.text: R. Akiva's service sequence protects the HP's stamina for the essential atonement service. Halachic reasoning about the HP's capacity informs how services are arranged, prioritizing the essential over the incidental.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume the scriptural order is inviolable. R. Akiva demonstrates that protecting the HP's capacity for the central service can legitimately inform how the full service is sequenced.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Mishnah Ch. 8 - five Yom Kippur afflictions listed
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: Chapter 8 begins with the five Yom Kippur afflictions and the service sequence including the mussaf offerings.

argumentFlow.label: Dispute: Rabbi Eliezer vs Rabbi Akiva on mussaf timing
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Eliezer vs Rabbi Akiva
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Eliezer: all mussaf with the morning tamid. Rabbi Akiva: one mussaf lamb first, then day's service, then the rest at the fifth immersion.

argumentFlow.label: Reason: Torat Kohanim sequence vs Numbers sequence
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Torat Kohanim (Leviticus) was written first - therefore the day's service (from Lev. 16) precedes the mussafin (from Numbers 29).

argumentFlow.label: Reason: 'Besides the sin-offering of atonement' - external goat preceded
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Numbers says 'besides the sin-offering of atonement' - implying the external goat follows the day's internal service.

argumentFlow.label: Reason R. Akiva: might tire the HP if all mussaf first
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Akiva
argumentFlow.text: If all mussaf offerings precede the day's service, the HP might become too tired to perform the essential atonement service - which is the priority.

argumentFlow.label: Concern: Might neglect the rest of the mussaf after day's service
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If only one mussaf is done first, he might neglect the remaining mussafin after the day's service. The Gemara responds: he is energetic for the day's service and will follow through.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Day's service is the priority; sequence follows Torat Kohanim first
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The day's service is the primary atonement work; both the Torat Kohanim sequence and the Numbers mussaf must be honored, requiring the split approach.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: All agree one ram of people's mussaf is done
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: All opinions agree regarding the people's ram of the mussaf - only the timing of the seven lambs is disputed.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: The ram mentioned in Torat Kohanim is distinct from Numbers
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The ram mentioned in Torat Kohanim (Lev. 16) is separate from the ram in Numbers - both are required.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Two rams are each required individually
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Both rams are required: one as a freewill offering to be accepted willingly, one as an obligatory offering to fulfill the requirement properly.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Sin-offering fats burned at third immersion
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The sin-offering fats (eimurim) are burned at the third immersion, together with the HP's ram and the people's ram.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Afternoon tamid at fifth immersion
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The afternoon tamid and remaining mussaf offerings are performed at the fifth immersion - the final service of the day.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: 'Besides the sin-offering of atonement' resolved
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The phrase 'besides the sin-offering of atonement' establishes the external mussaf goat's position after the internal Yom Kippur service.

halachic: mussaf timing dispute - R. Eliezer vs R. Akiva
Torat Kohanim sequence vs Numbers sequence
HP's physical strength as halachic consideration
theological: prioritizing the essential atonement service over secondary offerings
narrative: the HP's full day of service - five immersions, multiple offerings

requiresUnderstanding: five immersions and ten sanctifications of HP
Torat Kohanim vs Chumash Hamifkad sequence

misconceptions.misconception: The mussaf offerings are all performed at the same time on Yom Kippur
misconceptions.correction: Per Rabbi Akiva: one mussaf lamb is performed first with the morning tamid, then the day's essential service, then the remaining mussafin and rams at the third immersion, and the afternoon tamid at the fifth.

visualizableElements.description: HP's day: (1) morning tamid + one mussaf lamb, (2) day's service (Lev. 16), (3) rams + eimurim, (4) ladle and fire-pan removal, (5) remaining mussafin + afternoon tamid

quizSeeds.question: Why does Rabbi Akiva disagree with performing all the mussaf offerings before the day's service?
quizSeeds.answer: He is concerned that performing all the mussaf offerings first might tire the HP before he performs the essential Yom Kippur atonement service - which is the primary purpose of the day.

sugyot.finalRuling: Rabbi Akiva: one mussaf lamb first with the morning tamid, then the day's essential service, then the rams and eimurim, then remaining mussafin and afternoon tamid. The HP's strength must be protected for the atonement service.

rashiTranslations.he: מסוף הספר לתחילתו. למפרע: וכל כך למה. למה צריך לומר
rashiTranslations.en: From the end of the scroll to the beginning - backward.

rashiTranslations.he: יותר ממה שקריתי לפניכם כתוב כאן: שלא להוציא לעז על ספר
rashiTranslations.en: And why so much - why must he say 'more than what I have read is written here'?

rashiTranslations.he: תורה. כשרואין אותו קורא פרשה שלישית על פה יהו סבורים שספר
rashiTranslations.en: So as not to denigrate the Torah scroll - when they see him read the third portion by heart, they might think it is missing from the scroll.

rashiTranslations.he: תורה חסר אותה פרשה: מפני כבוד ציבור. שיהו מצפין ודוממין
rashiTranslations.en: For the honor of the congregation - so they wait in silence and expectation.
Bring another scroll - so it will be rolled to the third portion.
For the third portion.

rashiTranslations.he: שלא יאמרו חסר הוא: ברכה שאינה
rashiTranslations.en: Because of its defect - lest they say it is defective.
An unnecessary blessing - so he would need to go back and bless again.
As it is established - as we say them in prayer.
The Rabbis taught: the rest of the prayer etc. - as taught in the Mishnah, this is the petition and supplication before You for Your people Israel who need saving.

rashiTranslations.he: השנויה במשנה כך היא רנה תחינה
rashiTranslations.en: The rest of the prayer taught in the Mishnah is thus: a prayer, petition, and request before You for Your people Israel.
For they need saving.
To show its appearance to many - to display the beauty of the Torah scroll and the glory of its owner who took pains to beautify himself for the commandment.

rashiTranslations.he: לרבים. להראות נויו של ספר תורה
rashiTranslations.en: As it says (Ex. 15): 'This is my God and I will glorify Him' - beautify yourself before Him in commandments; a beautiful lulav, a beautiful Torah scroll.
On beautiful parchment, beautiful ink, a skilled scribe.
And from the day before Yom Kippur they brought them there.
One may not pass over commandments - lest you say this also constitutes 'passing over,' since he is leaving this scroll and going to look at that one.

rashiTranslations.he: נאה ספר תורה נאה בקלף נאה
rashiTranslations.en: And what commandment - what commandment.

rashiTranslations.he: בדיו נאה בלבלר אומן ומערב יום
rashiTranslations.en: Is there in seeing them? There is a commandment here because of 'in the multitude of people is the King's glory.'
Therefore it teaches us - that it is not 'passing over' since he is not currently engaged with it.

rashiTranslations.he: על המצות. ומהו דתימא האי נמי
rashiTranslations.en: Mishnah: seven lambs - of the mussaf, in the Book of Numbers (29).

rashiTranslations.he: אעבורי הוא שמניח זו והולך לראות
rashiTranslations.en: And the burnt-offering bull - also of the mussaf, together with the afternoon tamid.
In the fifth immersion.

rashiTranslations.he: בראייתן יש כאן מצוה משום ברוב עם הדרת
rashiTranslations.en: Gemara: What does he mean - Rabbi Akiva.

rashiTranslations.he: מלך: קמ"ל. דלאו מעבר הוא מאחר
rashiTranslations.en: And furthermore the burnt-offering bull of Rabbi Eliezer - who omitted it and did not discuss when he performs it.
The same difficulty applies to the goat as well, but he just mentioned the first item.
The sin-offering fats - as taught in another chapter: he placed them on a tray until he burns them.
You will not find it - the order of the day's service properly arranged in our Mishnah.

rashiTranslations.he: נמי דמוסף עם תמיד של בין הערבים
rashiTranslations.en: You will only find it properly arranged according to Rabbi Eliezer (Tanna of the school of Samuel) who discusses all of them.
Or according to Rabbi Akiva in the Tosefta where he explains his words well.

rashiTranslations.he: רבי עקיבא: ותו פר העולה לרבי
rashiTranslations.en: According to Rabbi Akiva we do not read 'he went out and performed.'
With the morning tamid - in the first vestment change.

rashiTranslations.he: אימת עביד ליה והוא הדין נמי
rashiTranslations.en: As stated about the mussakf of all holidays: 'besides the morning burnt-offering' - thus the mussafin are performed adjacent to the morning offering.
And we learn Yom Kippur from them.
And then the day's service.
And then the external goat - even though it is from the mussaf.
He cannot precede it to the day's service because it says 'a sin-offering goat, besides the sin-offering of atonement.'
We learn that the internal sin-offering goat preceded it.
And then.
The rams - his ram and the people's ram - and then the sin-offering fats.
All of these at the third immersion.

rashiTranslations.he: שפירש שם את דבריו יפה בדרבי
rashiTranslations.en: And then the afternoon tamid - at the fifth immersion.

rashiTranslations.he: עקיבא לא גרסינן יצא ועשה: עם
rashiTranslations.en: And we do not discuss here the removal of the ladle and fire-pan since it is obvious to us that it follows the rams.
As taught later - the entire portion was stated in order etc.
He performed as written - Torat Kohanim was written first.

rashiTranslations.he: עולת הבקר אלמא מוספין עם עולת
rashiTranslations.en: Therefore the rams and the sin-offering fats come after the day's service, then removal of the ladle and fire-pan.

rashiTranslations.he: הבקר נעשית בסמוך להן וילמוד יום
rashiTranslations.en: Then the mussafin and the afternoon tamid which are written last in Numbers.
This 'besides the sin-offering of atonement' - which implies the external goat precedes the rams and people's ram.
What does he do with it? Granted 'besides the morning burnt-offering' is not written for Yom Kippur itself.

rashiTranslations.he: ואף על פי שהוא מן המוסף לא
rashiTranslations.en: And the continuum-learner does not derive from it to perform these things from Torat Kohanim first.
But this 'besides the sin-offering of atonement' written for Yom Kippur itself - what does he do with it?
And then.
Rashi line 53: continuation.
Rashi line 54: continuation.
Rashi line 55: continuation.
